file_name: Non Disclosure Agreement - CP Specific NDA (CreativeNDA-CPS121.1014).txt

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representa tives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any inform ation that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affai rs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specificatio ns, trade secrets or so ftware of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Inform ation; but not including any info rmation: 1 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless betreated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non- confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Partyor otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient independently of the information disclosed by theDisclosing Pa rty.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means only the specific documents and materials explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by the Disclosing Party at the time of disclosure. This definition supersedes any other interpretation. For avoidance of doubt, information is only confidential if both parties agree in writing that it is considered confidential. Any oral statements do not qualify as a method of making any information confidential. Information will only be considered confidential if it is agreed in writing before its disclosure. If not, even the specific documents and materials explicitly marked as 'Confidential' will not be considered confidential if it is agreed in writing before its disclosure to the other party. Any other information, including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any inform ation that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affai rs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specificatio ns, trade secrets or so ftware of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Inform ation; but not including any info rmation: 1 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless betreated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non- confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Partyor otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient independently of the information disclosed by theDisclosing Pa rty.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad, encompassing various types of information disclosed. The modified definition severely restricts this to only information explicitly marked 'Confidential' in writing and agreed upon in writing before its disclosure. This introduces in-text contradiction with sections relying on the original, broader definition, rendering the enforcement of confidentiality obligations uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) not use or ex ploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this A greement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictlynecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be theproperty of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of theRecipient; (e) apply the same security measures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as theRecipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providingadequate p rotection from unauthorised disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the otherin tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' significantly narrows its scope compared to the original definition. The changed text limits 'Confidential Information' to only those documents and materials explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing and agreed upon in writing before its disclosure, superseding any other interpretation. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which obligates the recipient to protect a broader range of information disclosed by the disclosing party. The original definition includes business affairs, customer lists, and other sensitive information regardless of explicit marking. Therefore, the change creates a clear contradiction, making the recipient's confidentiality obligations outlined in the contradicted section unenforceable for information not explicitly marked and agreed upon in writing, thus answering YES.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Inform ation are granted to the other P arty and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this A greement. Any background intellectual property r ights subsisting in a ny information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non- exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, revocable licence to use such background Intellectual 4 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Purpose only. Any foreground intellec tual property r ights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest , right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 All Information, regardless if it has been agreed upon in writing before its disclosure or not, and not explicitly identified as confidential under section 1.1 (as modified) shall be considered public information. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Inform ation are granted to the other P arty and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this A greement. Any background intellectual property r ights subsisting in a ny information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non- exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, revocable licence to use such background Intellectual 4 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Purpose only. Any foreground intellec tual property r ights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest , right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.explanation: Section 4.1 now asserts that any information not explicitly marked as confidential under the restrictive new definition in 1.1 is public. This directly contradicts the remaining clauses that assume a broader understanding of 'Confidential Information' exists, effectively nullifying the intent of the NDA for any information not strictly meeting the written agreement requirements. This contradicts section 2.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) not use or ex ploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this A greement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictlynecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be theproperty of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of theRecipient; (e) apply the same security measures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as theRecipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providingadequate p rotection from unauthorised disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the otherin tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4.1 redefines 'Confidential Information' by narrowing its scope to only include information explicitly marked as confidential under the revised Section 1.1. This directly contradicts Section 2.1, which obligates the Recipient to protect a broader range of 'Confidential Information' without the strict requirement of explicit identification. Consequently, the modified 4.1 undermines the protection intended by 2.1, creating a strong in-text contradiction that affects the enforceability and intent of the NDA.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The Recipi ent may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to those of its Representatives who need to know this Confidential Information for the Purpose, provided that: (a) it info rms its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure; (b) it procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed tothem, comply with this Agreement as if they were the Recipient and, if the Disclosing Party so requests, procure that any relevant Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement withthe Disclosing Party on terms equivalent to those contained in this Agreement; and (c) it keeps a written record of these Representatives; and (d) it shall at all times be liable for the failure of any Representati ve to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The Recipi ent may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to those of its Representatives who need to know this Confidential Information for the Purpose; the requirement that the Recipient informs its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure is waived and removed; the requirement that the Recipient procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed to them, comply with this Agreement as if they were the Recipient and, if the Disclosing Party so requests, procure that any relevant Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement withthe Disclosing Party on terms equivalent to those contained in this Agreement is waived and removed; the requirement that the Recipient keeps a written record of these Representatives is waived and removed; the Recipient shall not at any times be liable for the failure of any Representati ve to comply with the terms of this Agreement. In other words, the Recipient has no responsibility to their representatives, and as a result, the representatives are not subject to any of the same rules of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 2.2 originally stipulates that the Recipient is responsible for ensuring its Representatives comply with the NDA. The change removes this responsibility entirely. This creates a contradiction because other sections (e.g., the indemnity clause) still imply that the Recipient is liable for breaches by its Representatives, although this section now states that there is no such liability. If the recipient can't control its representatives, but they are not subject to the NDA, then it will never be able to fulfill clause 2.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 2.2 explicitly waives and removes the Recipient's responsibility for their Representatives' compliance with the agreement. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which states that the Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against all liabilities arising from any breach of the Agreement by the Recipient 'and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient'. The change creates a direct conflict, as the Recipient is now not responsible for their representatives in 2.2, but is still liable for their actions in the indemnity clause.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Notices 11.1 Any notice or other communication r equired to be given under this Agreement, shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, or sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery or by commercial courier, to each P arty required to receive the notice or communication at its address as set out bel ow: CP: Shane E. Weyant, 2 14 Industrial Lane, Alum Bank, PA 15521 Company: _______________________________________________________________________ or as otherwise s pecified by the relevant Party by n otice in writing to each other Party. 11.2 Any notice or ot her communicati on shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) if delivered pers onally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in clause 11.1; or (b) if sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the second Business Day after post ing; or 6 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (c) if delivered by commercial courier, on the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed. 11.3 A notice or other c ommunication r equired to be given under this Agreement shall not be validly given if sent by e -mail.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Notices 11.1 Any notice or other communication r equired to be given under this Agreement, shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, or sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery or by commercial courier, or by email to each P arty required to receive the notice or communication at its address as set out bel ow: CP: Shane E. Weyant, 2 14 Industrial Lane, Alum Bank, PA 15521 Company: _______________________________________________________________________ or as otherwise s pecified by the relevant Party by n otice in writing to each other Party. 11.2 Any notice or ot her communicati on shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) if delivered pers onally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in clause 11.1; or (b) if sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the second Business Day after post ing; or 6 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (c) if delivered by commercial courier, on the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed; or (d) if sent by email, the time at which it was sent. 11.3 A notice or other c ommunication r equired to be given under this Agreement shall not be validly given if sent by e -mail.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 11 initially specifies that notices cannot be validly given via email. The modification in 11.1 introduces email as a valid method for delivering notices. 11.2(d) also states when email will be deemed received, which is the time it was sent. While this adds 'email', Clause 11.3 still explicitly states that email is *not* valid. This creates a direct and obvious contradiction about whether email is an acceptable form of communication under the agreement. The contradiction can also occur if any other clause depends on giving notice.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11.3 A notice or other c ommunication r equired to be given under this Agreement shall not be validly given if sent by e -mail.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Clause 11.1 introduces email as a valid method for delivering notices, and 11.2(d) states when an email will be deemed received. However, Clause 11.3, the contradicted text, explicitly states that a notice is not valid if sent by email. This creates a direct and obvious contradiction within Clause 11, about whether email is an acceptable form of communication under the agreement. The original text disallowed emails and the new text directly contradicts this, leading to a Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue indefinitely from the date of this A greement, unless both parties agree in writing to terminate it.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause 6.1 sets a fixed duration of 2 years for the confidentiality obligations. The modified version states that the obligations continue indefinitely unless both parties agree to terminate in writing. This contradicts any expectation of a finite term of obligation and impacts any clause that may consider the 2 year period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certi fy in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, orbased on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or anyapplicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipientto keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this A greement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 2-year duration for obligations under Clause 6.1. The changed text extends this obligation 'indefinitely'. The contradicted text describes the process of returning or destroying confidential information 'at the request of the Disclosing Party', implying a point at which such a request might reasonably be made, potentially after the initial 2-year period. By removing the finite term, the changed text contradicts the implicit assumption within the contradicted text that these obligations and data retention provisions will have an endpoint, thus making the action to completely wipe the data unclear, since the agreement continues indefinitely.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Variation Except as set out i n this Agreement, no variation of this Agreement, including the introduction of any additional terms and conditions, shall be effective unless it is agreed in writing and signed by both P arties.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Variation Except as set out i n this Agreement, no variation of this Agreement, including the introduction of any additional terms and conditions, shall be effective unless it is agreed in writing and signed by only one of the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original Clause 15 requires that any variation to the agreement must be signed by both parties to be effective. By changing this to require signature by only one party, it directly contradicts the principle of mutual agreement and consent that is fundamental to contract law. It also means that any other party can change the agreement as he wishes, not needing to consult with the other one to do it. It creates uncertainty about the enforceability of other clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreemen t constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them,whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Clause 15, which previously required both parties to sign any variation to the agreement. The change now stipulates that only one party's signature is required. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the agreement represents the entire understanding between the parties. By allowing one party to unilaterally alter the agreement, the changed text undermines the mutual agreement principle and the 'entire agreement' clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
